◆ Oral Presentation IEEE ISBI 2026 Int'l Symposium on Biomedical Imaging

AI Generalisation Gap in Comorbid Sleep Disorder Staging

Deep sleep-staging models reach state-of-the-art accuracy on healthy EEG, yet generalise poorly to clinical populations with disrupted sleep. Using Grad-CAM, we show the model attends to physiologically uninformative EEG regions in patient data — and we introduce iSLEEPS, a clinically annotated ischemic-stroke dataset.

Saswata Bose♦, Suvadeep Maiti♦♣, Shivam Kumar Sharma♦, Mythirayee S‡, Tapabrata Chakraborti†, Srijitesh Rajendran‡, Raju S. Bapi♦
♦International Institute of Information Technology (IIIT-H), Hyderabad, India  ·  †Alan Turing Institute & University College London, UK
♣Queen Square Institute of Neurology, University College London, UK  ·  ‡National Institute of Mental Health and Neurosciences (NIMHANS), Bangalore, India

The finding

State-of-the-art on healthy EEG — poor generalisation to patients.

55%
Accuracy of a model trained on healthy SHHS data when evaluated on the iSLEEPS ischemic-stroke cohort (macro-F1 51.9%). The paper reports an approximately 30% drop in cross-domain performance.

Accurate sleep staging is essential for diagnosing obstructive sleep apnea and hypopnea, both highly prevalent among stroke patients. Deep learning enables automated staging from raw EEG of healthy participants — but the paper shows most models fail to generalise to clinical populations with disrupted sleep architecture.

The same SE-ResNet + Bi-LSTM model reaches 74.7% accuracy when trained and tested on iSLEEPS, but only 55.1% when a model trained on healthy SHHS data is applied to the patient cohort. Grad-CAM, statistical, and computational analyses are used to characterise this gap.

TL;DR

Current sleep-stage classification models do not generalise between healthy and comorbid cohorts. Their detection of sleep stages becomes medically unreliable, and such models, in their current state, cannot be used without medical supervision.

In-domain vs cross-domain accuracy
Values from Table 1 alongside the cross-domain result reported in the paper. The same architecture reaches 74.7% when trained on iSLEEPS, but 55.1% when a healthy-trained (SHHS) model is deployed on it.

▼ ≈30% drop in cross-domain performance (SHHS → iSLEEPS), as reported in the paper
Abstract

Accurate sleep staging is essential for diagnosing disorders like obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) and hypopnea, both highly prevalent among stroke patients. Conventional polysomnography (PSG), though clinically reliable, is labor-intensive, costly, and reliant on manual scoring. Recent advances in deep learning enable automated staging from raw EEG of healthy participants. However, our investigations reveal that most models fail to generalize to clinical populations with disrupted sleep architecture. This paper is meant to demonstrate this phenomenon in a structured manner, highlighting the issue through Grad-CAM based interpretations.

To do this, we introduce iSLEEPS, a new clinically annotated ischemic stroke dataset, and evaluate a deep learning framework combining SE-ResNet feature extraction with bidirectional LSTM layers for contextual sleep stage classification using single-channel EEG. As expected, the model indicates poor cross-domain generalization between healthy and diseased patients. Comparison of the attention visualisations with expert clinical feedback reveals that this might be due to the model attending to physiologically uninformative EEG regions in patient data. Further supportive statistical and computational analyses confirm significant differences in sleep architecture between healthy and ischemic stroke subjects. These findings highlight the need for subject-aware or disease-specific sleep staging models that focus on medically relevant features, alongside clinical validation of automated tagging before integration into treatment workflows.

Contributions

What this paper contributes.

01 — Dataset

iSLEEPS

Introduced iSLEEPS, a new PSG dataset of 100 ischemic-stroke patients with severe sleep disorders.

02 — Model

Benchmark model

Developed a benchmarking deep-learning model for iSLEEPS that achieves state-of-the-art performance and reveals the limited generalisation of models trained on healthy cohorts.

03 — Analysis

Clinician-validated analysis

Statistical and explainability analyses, validated by clinician feedback, showed deep models do not generalise between healthy and comorbid cohorts — underscoring the need for subject-aware or disease-specific models.

Method

Architecture

The model classifies each 30-second EEG epoch using a sliding window of neighbouring epochs for temporal context. An SE-ResNet extracts features from the raw single-channel EEG, bidirectional LSTM layers encode the sequence, and a linear classification head predicts the sleep stage (W, N1, N2, N3, REM).

Datasets

Three healthy cohorts and one patient cohort.

All models are evaluated on raw single-channel EEG.

Explainability

The generalisation gap, seen through Grad-CAM.

On the iSLEEPS cohort, a model trained on healthy SHHS data reaches only 55.1% accuracy (macro-F1 51.9%). Comparing the attention visualisations with expert clinical feedback suggests this might be because the model attends to physiologically uninformative EEG regions in patient data. In the example below, one epoch is correctly tagged with attention on the alpha waves present in the signal, while another is misclassified — predicting REM for a true N2 epoch — despite the K-complexes and sleep spindles it contains.

In contrast, a model trained and tested on iSLEEPS attends to the K-complex and spindles that define the stage — the medically relevant features a clinician would use.

Sleep architecture

Data-level differences between the cohorts.

On a balanced set of SleepEDF-78 (healthy) and iSLEEPS (patient) epochs, statistical and computational analyses confirm significant differences in sleep architecture between the two cohorts.

≈0.17
KL divergence
between transition maps
1831.5
χ² statistic
(19 degrees of freedom)
13 / 20
Transitions differing
significantly
100%
Classifier accuracy
separating the cohorts
The statistical lens — stage transitions

The probability of transitioning between stages differs between the cohorts. For example, the probability of moving from N2 to Wake is higher in the stroke cohort (0.354) than in healthy sleepers (0.164). The transition graphs below show the probabilities of moving between sleep stages for each dataset, excluding insignificant and self-transitions.

The computational lens — separability

Binary classifiers — Random Forest, Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, and SVM — were trained to distinguish patient from healthy recordings using 27 structural and sequential features. The Average Run Length (the mean number of consecutive 30-second epochs in a single stage) separates the two cohorts perfectly, with 100% accuracy and zero AUC-ROC variance across cross-validation folds.

Ethics & acknowledgements

Ethical compliance

The study was carried out at NIMHANS, Bengaluru, India, a leading tertiary care referral teaching hospital. Approval was obtained from the NIMHANS Institutional Ethics Committee No. NIMHANS/34th IEC (BS&NS DIV.)/2022 dated 05.02.2022. Ethical considerations were strictly followed throughout the study.

Acknowledgements

We acknowledge IHub-Data, IIIT Hyderabad (H1-002), for financial assistance. Tapabrata Chakraborti is supported by the Turing-Roche Strategic Partnership and the UCL NIHR Biomedical Research Centre.

Sleep-stage models do not yet generalise between healthy and comorbid cohorts; the paper calls for clinical validation before deployment.
